title: Wavelength and frequency for sound waves  in tubes
description: Practicing calculating the wavelength and frequency of sound waves created in open and closed tubes.
topic_path: Wavelength and frequency for sound waves  in tubes

assessment_items.question_content:
A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near the open end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next highest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.

We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $3$ times, so this must be the $3^\text {rd}$ harmonic.  


Since a closed tube can only have odd-numbered harmonics, the next highest harmonic is the $5^\text {th}$ harmonic.  The $5^\text {th}$harmonic contains a quarter of a wavelength $5$ times inside the tube, so the wavelength is $\dfrac{4}{5}L$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is $\dfrac{4}{5}L$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near one end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next highest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is an open tube, so it can have both odd- and even-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for an open tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many half wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a half of a wavelength $2$ times, so this must be the $2^\text {nd}$ harmonic.  


Since an open tube can have both odd- and even-numbered harmonics, the next highest harmonic is the $3^\text {rd}$ harmonic.  The $3^\text {rd}$  harmonic contains a half of a wavelength $3$ times inside the tube, so the wavelength is $\dfrac{2}{3}L$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is $\dfrac{2}{3}L$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near one end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next lowest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is an open tube, so it can have both odd- and even-numbered harmonics.  The fundamental wavelength for an open tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

 We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many half wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a half of a wavelength $2$ times, so this must be the $2^\text {nd}$ harmonic.  


Since an open tube can have both odd and even-numbered harmonics, the next lowest harmonic is the $1^\text {st}$ harmonic.  The $1^\text {st}$ harmonic contains a half of a wavelength once inside the tube, so the wavelength is $2L$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is $2L$.

A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near the open end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next lowest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

Since a closed tube can only have odd-numbered harmonics, the next lowest harmonic is the $1^\text {st}$ harmonic.  The $1^\text {st}$ harmonic contains a quarter of a wavelength once inside the tube, so the wavelength is $4L$.

The correct answer is $4L$.

A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near the open end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next lowest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $5$ times, so this must be the $5^\text {th}$  harmonic.  


Since a closed tube can only have odd-numbered harmonics, the next lowest harmonic is the $3^\text {rd}$ harmonic.  The $3^\text{rd}$ harmonic contains a quarter of a wavelength thrice inside the tube, so the wavelength is $\dfrac{4}{3}L$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is $\dfrac{4}{3}L$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tube of length $L$ is filled with air.  A standing sound wave is created by holding a tuning fork near the open end of the tube. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wavelength of the next lowest harmonic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $7$ times, so this must be the $7^\text{th}$  harmonic.  


Since a closed tube can only have odd-numbered harmonics, the next lowest harmonic is the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic.  The $5^\text{th}$ harmonic contains a quarter of a wavelength five times inside the tube, so the wavelength is $\dfrac{4}{5}L$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is $\dfrac{4}{5}L$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $1000\, \text{Hz}$ near the open end of a tube.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following frequencies can also exist for standing waves in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $5$ times, so this must be the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in a closed tube have frequencies that are odd numbered integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic is $5$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_5}{5}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{1000\,\text{Hz}}{5}
\\\\
f_1&=200\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The correct answer must be an odd numbered integer multiple of $200\,\text{Hz}$.
The correct answer is $200\,\text{Hz}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $1000\, \text{Hz}$ near the end of an open tube.  


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following frequencies can also exist for standing waves in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is an open tube, so it can have both odd and even-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for an open tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many half wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a half of a wavelength $2$ times, so this must be the $2^\text {nd}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in an open tube have frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the $2^\text {nd}$ harmonic is $2$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_2}{2}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{1000\,\text{Hz}}{2}
\\\\
f_1&=500\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The correct answer must be an integer multiple of $500\,\text{Hz}$.
The correct answer is $500\,\text{Hz}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $1000\, \text{Hz}$ near the open end of a tube.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which is the next highest frequency that can also exist for a standing wave in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.


We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $5$ times, so this must be the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in a closed tube have frequencies that are odd numbered integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic is $5$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_5}{n}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{1000\,\text{Hz}}{5}
\\\\
f_1&=200\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The next odd-numbered harmonic after the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic is the $7^\text{th}$ harmonic. It's frequency is:

 $7\cdot 200\,\text{Hz}=1400\,\text{Hz}$
The correct answer is $1400\,\text{Hz}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $1000\, \text{Hz}$ near the open end of a tube.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which is the next highest frequency that can also exist for a standing wave in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is an open tube, so it can have both odd and even-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for an open tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many half wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a half of a wavelength $2$ times, so this must be the  $2^\text{nd}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in an open tube have frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the  $2^\text{nd}$ harmonic is $2$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_2}{2}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{1000\,\text{Hz}}{2}
\\\\
f_1&=500\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The next harmonic after the $2^\text{nd}$ harmonic is the $3^\text{rd}$ harmonic. It's frequency is:

 $3\cdot 500\,\text{Hz}=1500\,\text{Hz}$
The correct answer is $1500\,\text{Hz}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $900\, \text{Hz}$ near the open end of a tube.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which is the next lowest frequency that can also exist for a standing wave in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is an open tube, so it can have both odd and even-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for an open tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$.

We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many half wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a half of a wavelength $3$ times, so this must be the  $3^\text{rd}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in an open tube have frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the  $3^\text{rd}$ harmonic is $3$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_3}{3}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{900\,\text{Hz}}{3}
\\\\
f_1&=300\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The next lower harmonic after the $3^\text{rd}$ harmonic is the $2^\text{nd}$ harmonic. It's frequency is:

 $2\cdot 300\,\text{Hz}=600\,\text{Hz}$
The correct answer is $600\,\text{Hz}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A standing sound wave is created by holding a vibrating tuning fork with frequency $4200\, \text{Hz}$ near the open end of a tube.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which is the next lowest frequency that can also exist for a standing wave in this tube? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is a closed tube, so it can only have odd-numbered harmonics. The fundamental wavelength for a closed tube is $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$.

We can figure out which harmonic is pictured by counting how many quarter wavelengths (or fundamental wavelengths) fit in the tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

This harmonic has a quarter of a wavelength $7$ times, so this must be the $7^\text{th}$ harmonic.  

Standing wave harmonics in a closed tube have frequencies that are odd numbered integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Thus, the $7^\text{th}$ harmonic is $7$ times the fundamental frequency, which is:

$\begin{align}f_n& = nf_1
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{f_7}{7}
\\\\
f_1&=\dfrac{4200\,\text{Hz}}{7}
\\\\
f_1&=600\,\text{Hz}\end{align}$

The next lower odd-numbered harmonic after the $7^\text{th}$ harmonic is the $5^\text{th}$ harmonic. It's frequency is:

 $5\cdot 600\,\text{Hz}=3000\,\text{Hz}$
The correct answer is $3000\,\text{Hz}$.
